title: 8 Essential Tools for a Professional-Style Home Pho Night
meta_description: Discover the 8 essential homemade pho tools you need for a restaurant‑quality pho night at home, from broth simmering to garnish prep.
html:
When you set out to make authentic, restaurant‑style pho at home, the right equipment can turn a long, labor‑intensive process into a streamlined, enjoyable experience. Below are the eight must‑have homemade pho tools that will help you achieve clear broth, perfectly cooked noodles, and picture‑perfect garnishes every time.

1. A Heavy‑Duty Stockpot for a Deep, Clear Broth

A 12‑quart stainless‑steel stockpot with a tight‑fitting lid is the foundation of any great pho. The thick walls distribute heat evenly, preventing hot spots that can cause a cloudy broth. When the broth is simmering, keep an eye on the surface and skim any foam with a slotted spoon to maintain that crystal‑clear appearance.

2. Fine‑Mesh Strainer and Onion Holder & Slicing Guide

After simmering beef or chicken bones for hours, you’ll need to strain the broth to remove bones, aromatics, and any impurities. A fine‑mesh strainer does the job without tearing the silk‑like texture of the liquid.

While the broth cooks, use the Onion Holder & Slicing Guide (2 PCS) to slice onions, ginger, and scallions uniformly. The stainless‑steel prongs hold the vegetable steady, giving you consistent pieces for the broth and reducing the risk of knife slips.

3. Mandoline Slicer for Thin Rice Noodles and Veggies

Rice noodles are the heart of pho, and a Mandoline Slicer helps you achieve the perfect thickness when you make them from scratch. Set the blade to a thin, uniform slice and glide the rice flour dough across the surface for even ribbons.

Use the same mandoline to prep thin slices of carrots, daikon, or zucchini for garnish. The built‑in hand guard keeps your fingers safe while you work quickly.

4. Garlic Press and Herb Scissors for Fresh Aromatics

Fresh garlic and cilantro are essential flavor boosters. A sturdy Garlic Press crushes cloves in seconds, releasing more volatile oils than a knife does. Follow with the Herb Scissors to mince cilantro, basil, and mint in one snip—no chopping board required.

5. Vertical Gyro Pan with Skewer for Perfectly Cooked Meats

Whether you’re using thinly sliced beef, chicken, or pork, the Vertical Gyro Pan with Skewer gives you that restaurant‑style char. Place the meat on the skewer, set the pan in the oven at 425°F, and let the heat rotate the meat for even cooking. The result is tender, slightly caramelized slices that slide easily onto the noodle bowl.

6. Taco Holder Stand for Organized Garnish Assembly

Pho is traditionally served with a platter of fresh herbs, lime wedges, bean sprouts, and sliced jalapeños. A Taco Holder Stand keeps each garnish upright and within arm’s reach, making the assembly line smooth and mess‑free. Your guests can add what they like without digging through a cluttered plate.

7. Instant‑Read Meat Thermometer for Precise Doneness

When you’re adding thinly sliced beef that will cook quickly in the hot broth, you want to guarantee it’s safe but not overcooked. An Instant‑Read Meat Thermometer gives you an exact temperature in seconds, ensuring the meat reaches at least 145°F before serving.

8. Presentation Extras: Cocktail Umbrellas & Picks

Pho night can feel like a special occasion with a few fun touches. Scatter a few 150‑Pack Cocktail Drink Umbrellas & Picks among the garnish plates. The mini parasols add a splash of color and a playful vibe—perfect for a backyard gathering or a cozy dinner with friends.

With these eight essential homemade pho tools, you’ll be able to master every step of the process—from broth to bowl—without breaking a sweat. The right equipment not only saves time but also elevates the flavor and presentation, turning a simple dinner into a memorable culinary experience.

FAQ

Do I really need a vertical gyro pan for pho?

While you can cook thinly sliced meat on a skillet, the vertical gyro pan provides consistent heat and a slight char that mimics the street‑food experience. It’s especially useful when cooking larger batches.

Can I use a regular kitchen knife instead of the onion holder?

You can, but the onion holder & slicing guide offers safer, uniform cuts and frees up both hands for other prep tasks, which speeds up the overall workflow.

What’s the best way to store leftover broth?

Cool the broth quickly in an ice‑water bath, then transfer it to airtight containers. Freeze in 1‑cup portions for easy reheating. A fine‑mesh strainer helps remove any remaining solids before storage.
